content_en:
A tribe was a group of people who were linked to one another through blood relation. It was the laws and customs of each tribe that dictated the relation between the individual and the group as well as the rights that were due to and obligatory upon - depending on the case - each member of the tribe. For example, the chief of a tribe had many rights over his people, but they too had rights over him. There were a number of factors that made a tribe member suitable for the position of chief - his status, generosity, character, bravery, and so on. The chief of a given tribe had the right to be honoured and obeyed by his people; if there was a dispute, his judgment was final. He had monetary rights as

well: one-quarter of the spoils of war belonged to him; before the distribution of the spoils of war, he had the right to choose specific items for himself; whatever was taken from an enemy before battle belonged to him; whatever form of wealth (from the spoils of war) could not be distributed, belonged to him. These rights did not come without a price; the chief had many duties that he had to fulfill, some of which effectively nullified the benefits of the aforementioned monetary rights. During times of peace, he was expected to be very generous to his fellow tribesmen. And in war, he was expected to fight in the frontlines; also, it was his duty and right to enact peace agreements when doing so was for the benefit of the tribe.

Living under tribal laws and customs, the average tribesman lived a life of freedom, having to answer to no one, as long as he harmed no one. And as such, it became a part of the nature of Arabs to love freedom, and to hate injustice and subservience. Every individual member of a given tribe fought for all of the other members; more honour for the tribe meant more honour for the individual tribesman. On the downside of this arrangement, one automatically supported his fellow tribesmen, regardless of whether they were in the right or in the wrong. Thus in some regards, the individual tribesman was considered more as a member of the tribe than as an individual.

Each individual tribe had its own status and political reality, and depending on its situation, it waged war against or formed alliances with other tribes. War was a constant reality among Arabs; among the most famous of wars in their history was the War of Fijaar. But other than major wars, minor skirmishes or attacks were commonplaces. A member of one tribe might attack a member of another tribe for personal motives, such as revenge; or one tribe might attack another tribe for profit; the livelihood of some tribes actually depended upon the practice of attacking other tribes and taking all of their possessions by force. After such ruthless attacks took place, homes and entire villages were left empty, as if no one had lived there the day before.

Politics, religion, Economics, and the overall condition of Arabs... 41

The Economic Situation Of The Arabian Peninsula

Since most of the Arabian Peninsula consists of vast desert land, its inhabitants did not work in agriculture, except in the extreme borders of the Peninsula - particularly in Yemen, to the south, and Syria, to the north - and in the odd scattered oases found in central Arabia. Without much vegetation in Arabia, it was sheep and camels that made for the livelihood of both desert and city dwellers. Tribes would go from place to place with their herds, looking for pastureland.

As for industry and manufacturing, Arabs were far, far behind other nations; they practically refused to work in those fields, instead allowing foreigners and slaves to do their work for them. Even when they wanted to rebuild the Ka’bah, they sought the help of an Egyptian, who had survived the sinking of his sea vessel in Jeddah, and who then settled in Makkah.

It is true that, without farming and manufacturing, Arabs were, compared to other peoples, at an economic disadvantage; but they did compensate in another regard: They were an advanced trading nation, by dint of their strategically sound location between Africa and eastern Asia.

It was primarily the city dwellers of the Arabian Peninsula who engaged in trade, and most successful among them were the people of the Quraish. The Quraish differed from other tribes in that they were the inhabitants of Makkah, which was considered to be holy by all Arabs; as such, they were able to travel in safety all over Arabia, for no tribe dared to attack the dwellers of the inviolable city of Makkah. Other tribes did not fare as well; their trading caravans were constantly being subjected to highway robbery by individuals and others tribes that made a living through attacking and robbing passing caravans, Allah 3 reminded the Quraish of this particular blessing in the Noble Qur'an:

of 7 4 oh

LH 64 oe 4@ GK Al thas OAS

“Have they not seen that We have made (Makkah) a sanctuary secure, and that men are being snatched away from all around them? Then do they believe in Batil (falsehood - polytheism, idols, and all deities other than Allah), and deny (become ingrate for) the Graces of Allah?” (Qur’an 29: 67)

The Quraish dispatched two very large trading caravans on a yearly basis; one went in the winter to Yemen, and the other went in the summer to Ash-Sham (Syria and surrounding regions). They went in safety, while other tribes were constantly being attacked and robbed. Throughout every year, the Quraish sent many lesser caravans to the various marketplaces of Arabia (and perhaps even elsewhere). Allah 36 said:

Bs 35 EO wah Ear t, ah O 353 aly>

40 ph & MOG ge ot AE oa oti

“(It is a great Grace and Protection from Allah), for the taming of the Quraish, (and with all those Allah's Grace and Protections for their taming , We cause) the (Quraish) caravans to set forth safe in winter (to the south), and in summer (to the north without any fear), So let them worship (Allah) the Lord of this House (the Ka'bah in Makkah) . (He) Who has fed them against hunger, and has made them safe from fear .’’ (Qur’an 106: 1-4)

The caravans carried all kinds of merchandise that was available in the Arabian Peninsula ~ such as perfume, incense, spices, dates, ivory, beads, skins, silk garments, and weapons. Some merchandise was produced in the Peninsula, but some was imported from abroad. The trading caravans would carry such items to Ash-Sham and elsewhere, and would then return with full loads of wheat, grains, raisins, oils, and clothing.

The Yemenis were also known for trading, for their economic activities were conducted on land and by sea; they traveled to the shores of Africa, India, Indonesia, Sumatra, and the islands of the Arabian Peninsula. Once the inhabitants of Yemen became Muslims, they used their previous travel experience and

Politics, religion, Economics, and the overall condition of Arabs... 43

knowledge to help spread Islam to the above-mentioned lands.

Prior to the advent of Islam, usury was practiced on a widespread scale, perhaps having come to the Arabs from the Jews. In some cases, interest rates reached as high as one-hundred percent.

‘Ukaadh, Majinnah, and Dhul-Majaaz were the names of the most famous marketplaces of the Arabian Peninsula. Some historians relate that Arabs would congregate at ‘Ukaadh at the beginning of Dhil-Qai’dah; after twenty days passed, they would go to Majinnah. And when they saw the birth of the new moon for Dhil-Hijjah, they would go to Dhul-Majaaz, where they would stay for 8 days. Then they would go to ‘Arafah for the pilgrimage. And neither in ‘Arafah nor during the days of Minah did they conduct any business - not until the advent of Islam, for Allah 34 permitted them to do business ss during th those meee

ae ae Sie aif at galaye 4 A

“There is no sin on you wif you seek f the Bounty of your Lord (during pilgrimage by trading, etc.). Then when you leave ‘Arafat, remember Allah (by glorifying His Praises, i.e., prayers and invocations, etc.) at the Mash’ar-il-Haraam. And remember Him (by invoking Allah for all good, etc.) as He has guided you, and verily, you were, before, of those who were astray.” (Qur'an

These centers of trade remained open during the early days of Islam, but eventually closed down. During their heyday, ‘Ukaadh, Majinnah, and Dhul-Majaaz were not merely marketplaces; they were also centers of poetry and public speaking. Great poets and speakers gathered and competed against one another in their respective arts; thus they were centers that greatly served the advancement of poetry and the Arabic

Like most primitive cultures, customs and traditions passed down through the generations dictated the social norms of Arabs, the social status of individual tribesman, the relations between fellow tribesmen, and the relations between one tribe and another. The following are some of the basic realities of Arab society prior to the advent of Islam:

1) There was no limit to the degree to which the nobility felt proud about their ancestry and ranking in society

Arabs were obsessed with the idea of preserving their pure blood, and so they would not intermarry with other races. Islam brought an end to that, making it clear to Arabs that the only factors that make one person superior to the next are piety and good deeds.

2) Arabs were also obsessed with eloquent speech and purity of language

Perhaps the reason why poetry had a strong hold on the hearts and minds of Arabs is that it was the most beautiful way of expressing and recording for posterity - when a poem was especially good - facts about their noble lineages. It is not surprising, therefore, that Arab societies produced so many wonderful poets and public speakers. A single line of poetry had the potential of raising or lowering the status of a tribe. So just as a modern day country becomes thrilled when its favourite sporting team performs at a world-class level, an Arab tribe would become overjoyed if one of its members became a skilled poet.

3) Women were treated like merchandise

At least among most tribes, women were on an equal par with merchandise, in a very literal sense, for they were passed down through inheritance. If a man died, his wife was passed down to his eldest son - provided, of course, that she was not his actual mother. He then had the right to marry her, or even to prevent her

Politics, religion, Economics, and the overall condition of Arabs... 45
content_id:
Sebuah suku adalah kelompok orang-orang yang terhubung satu sama lain melalui hubungan darah. Hukum dan adat setiap suku yang mengatur hubungan antara individu dan kelompok, serta hak-hak yang menjadi milik dan kewajiban — tergantung pada kasusnya — setiap anggota suku. Misalnya, kepala suku memiliki banyak hak atas rakyatnya, namun mereka pun memiliki hak atas dirinya. Terdapat sejumlah faktor yang membuat seorang anggota suku layak menduduki posisi kepala suku — status, kedermawanan, karakter, keberanian, dan sebagainya. Kepala suku berhak untuk dihormati dan ditaati oleh rakyatnya; jika terjadi perselisihan, keputusannya bersifat final. Ia memiliki hak-hak finansial pula:

seperempat dari harta rampasan perang menjadi miliknya; sebelum pembagian harta rampasan perang, ia berhak memilih barang-barang tertentu untuk dirinya sendiri; apa pun yang diambil dari musuh sebelum pertempuran menjadi miliknya; segala bentuk kekayaan (dari harta rampasan perang) yang tidak dapat dibagi, menjadi miliknya. Hak-hak ini tidak datang tanpa imbalan; kepala suku memiliki banyak kewajiban yang harus ia penuhi, yang sebagiannya secara efektif meniadakan manfaat dari hak-hak finansial yang disebutkan di atas. Di masa damai, ia diharapkan untuk sangat dermawan kepada sesama anggota sukunya. Dan dalam peperangan, ia diharapkan bertempur di barisan terdepan; juga, menjadi kewajiban dan haknya untuk membuat perjanjian damai ketika hal itu menguntungkan suku.

Hidup di bawah hukum dan adat kesukuan, anggota suku biasa menjalani kehidupan yang bebas, tidak perlu mempertanggungjawabkan sesuatu kepada siapa pun, selama ia tidak menyakiti siapa pun. Oleh karena itu, mencintai kebebasan dan membenci ketidakadilan serta perbudakan menjadi bagian dari watak bangsa Arab. Setiap anggota suku berjuang demi seluruh anggota lainnya; semakin terhormat sukunya, semakin terhormat pula individu anggotanya. Sisi negatif dari tatanan ini, seseorang secara otomatis mendukung sesama anggota sukunya, tanpa memandang apakah mereka benar atau salah. Dengan demikian, dalam beberapa hal, anggota suku individu lebih dipandang sebagai bagian dari suku daripada sebagai individu.

Setiap suku memiliki status dan realitas politiknya sendiri, dan tergantung situasinya, ia melancarkan perang atau membentuk aliansi dengan suku-suku lain. Perang adalah kenyataan yang terus-menerus di kalangan bangsa Arab; di antara perang paling terkenal dalam sejarah mereka adalah Perang Fijar. Namun selain perang besar, bentrokan kecil atau serangan sudah menjadi hal yang lumrah. Seorang anggota suku mungkin menyerang anggota suku lain karena motif pribadi, seperti balas dendam; atau satu suku mungkin menyerang suku lain untuk mencari keuntungan; mata pencaharian sebagian suku bahkan bergantung pada praktik menyerang suku-suku lain dan merampas seluruh harta mereka dengan paksa. Setelah serangan brutal semacam itu terjadi, rumah-rumah dan seluruh desa ditinggalkan kosong, seolah tidak ada yang pernah tinggal di sana sehari sebelumnya.

Politik, Agama, Ekonomi, dan Kondisi Umum Bangsa Arab... 41

Situasi Ekonomi Jazirah Arab

Karena sebagian besar Jazirah Arab terdiri dari hamparan padang pasir yang luas, penduduknya tidak bergerak di bidang pertanian, kecuali di perbatasan terluar Semenanjung — khususnya di Yaman, di selatan, dan Syam, di utara — serta di oasis-oasis yang tersebar di Arabia tengah. Tanpa banyak tumbuh-tumbuhan di Arabia, domba dan unta menjadi sumber penghidupan bagi para penghuni padang pasir maupun kota. Suku-suku berpindah dari satu tempat ke tempat lain bersama ternak mereka, mencari padang penggembalaan.

Adapun dalam hal industri dan manufaktur, bangsa Arab jauh, jauh tertinggal dari bangsa-bangsa lain; mereka praktis menolak bekerja di bidang tersebut, dan menyerahkan pekerjaan itu kepada orang asing dan budak. Bahkan ketika mereka ingin membangun kembali Ka'bah, mereka meminta bantuan seorang Mesir yang selamat dari tenggelamnya kapalnya di Jeddah, lalu menetap di Makkah.

Benar bahwa tanpa pertanian dan manufaktur, bangsa Arab berada pada posisi yang kurang menguntungkan secara ekonomi dibandingkan bangsa-bangsa lain; namun mereka mengimbanginya dalam aspek lain: Mereka adalah bangsa pedagang yang maju, berkat letak geografis mereka yang strategis di antara Afrika dan Asia timur.

Terutama penduduk kota di Jazirah Arab yang terlibat dalam perdagangan, dan yang paling sukses di antara mereka adalah kaum Quraisy. Quraisy berbeda dari suku-suku lain karena mereka adalah penghuni Makkah, yang dianggap suci oleh seluruh bangsa Arab; dengan demikian, mereka dapat bepergian dengan aman ke seluruh penjuru Arabia, karena tidak ada suku yang berani menyerang penghuni kota Makkah yang mulia. Suku-suku lain tidak seberuntung itu; kafilah dagang mereka senantiasa menjadi sasaran perampokan oleh individu-individu dan suku-suku lain yang mencari nafkah dengan menyerang dan merampok kafilah yang lewat. Allah mengingatkan kaum Quraisy akan karunia khusus ini dalam Al-Qur'an yang Mulia:

of 7 4 oh

LH 64 oe 4@ GK Al thas OAS

"Apakah mereka tidak memperhatikan bahwa Kami telah menjadikan (Makkah) tanah suci yang aman, padahal manusia di sekelilingnya saling merampok? Apakah (nikmat) yang batil yang mereka percayai dan nikmat Allah mereka ingkari?" (Qur'an 29: 67)

Kaum Quraisy mengirimkan dua kafilah dagang yang sangat besar setiap tahunnya; satu berangkat di musim dingin menuju Yaman, dan yang lainnya berangkat di musim panas menuju Ash-Syam (Suriah dan wilayah sekitarnya). Mereka pergi dalam keamanan, sementara suku-suku lain senantiasa diserang dan dirampok. Sepanjang tahun, Quraisy mengirimkan banyak kafilah yang lebih kecil ke berbagai pasar Arabia (dan mungkin juga ke tempat lain). Allah berfirman:

Bs 35 EO wah Ear t, ah O 353 aly>

40 ph & MOG ge ot AE oa oti

"Karena kebiasaan orang-orang Quraisy, (yaitu) kebiasaan mereka bepergian pada musim dingin dan musim panas. Maka hendaklah mereka menyembah Tuhan (Allah) Pemilik rumah ini (Ka'bah). Yang telah memberi mereka makanan untuk menghilangkan lapar dan mengamankan mereka dari rasa takut." (Qur'an 106: 1-4)

Kafilah-kafilah itu membawa berbagai macam barang dagangan yang tersedia di Jazirah Arab — seperti wewangian, kemenyan, rempah-rempah, kurma, gading, manik-manik, kulit, pakaian sutra, dan senjata. Sebagian barang dagangan diproduksi di Semenanjung, namun sebagian lagi diimpor dari luar negeri. Kafilah dagang membawa barang-barang tersebut ke Ash-Syam dan tempat-tempat lain, lalu kembali dengan muatan penuh berupa gandum, biji-bijian, kismis, minyak, dan pakaian.

Bangsa Yaman juga dikenal sebagai pedagang, karena kegiatan ekonomi mereka dilakukan di darat maupun di laut; mereka berlayar ke pantai-pantai Afrika, India, Indonesia, Sumatra, dan pulau-pulau di Jazirah Arab. Setelah penduduk Yaman memeluk Islam, mereka memanfaatkan pengalaman perjalanan dan

Politik, Agama, Ekonomi, dan Kondisi Umum Bangsa Arab... 43

pengetahuan mereka sebelumnya untuk membantu menyebarkan Islam ke negeri-negeri yang disebutkan di atas.

Sebelum datangnya Islam, riba dipraktikkan secara meluas, mungkin dibawa kepada bangsa Arab oleh kaum Yahudi. Dalam beberapa kasus, tingkat bunga mencapai setinggi seratus persen.

'Ukaadz, Majinnah, dan Dzul-Majaz adalah nama-nama pasar paling terkenal di Jazirah Arab. Sebagian sejarawan menyebutkan bahwa bangsa Arab berkumpul di 'Ukaadz pada awal bulan Dzil-Qa'dah; setelah dua puluh hari berlalu, mereka pergi ke Majinnah. Dan ketika mereka menyaksikan lahirnya bulan baru Dzil-Hijjah, mereka pergi ke Dzul-Majaz, di mana mereka tinggal selama 8 hari. Kemudian mereka pergi ke 'Arafah untuk berhaji. Dan baik di 'Arafah maupun selama hari-hari di Mina, mereka tidak melakukan transaksi bisnis apa pun — tidak sampai datangnya Islam, karena Allah mengizinkan mereka berbisnis selama hari-hari tersebut.

ae ae Sie aif at galaye 4 A

"Tidak ada dosa bagimu untuk mencari karunia (rezeki hasil perniagaan) dari Tuhanmu. Maka apabila kamu telah bertolak dari 'Arafah, berdzikirlah kepada Allah di Masy'aril Haram. Dan berdzikirlah kepada-Nya sebagaimana Dia telah memberi petunjuk kepadamu, sekalipun sebelumnya kamu benar-benar termasuk orang-orang yang tidak mengetahui." (Qur'an)

Pusat-pusat perdagangan ini tetap buka selama awal-awal Islam, namun akhirnya tutup. Pada masa kejayaannya, 'Ukaadz, Majinnah, dan Dzul-Majaz bukan sekadar pasar; mereka juga merupakan pusat puisi dan pidato. Para penyair dan orator besar berkumpul dan bersaing satu sama lain dalam seni mereka masing-masing; dengan demikian, tempat-tempat ini adalah pusat yang sangat berperan dalam kemajuan puisi dan bahasa Arab.

Seperti kebanyakan budaya primitif, adat dan tradisi yang diwariskan turun-temurun mengatur norma-norma sosial bangsa Arab, status sosial anggota suku individu, hubungan antara sesama anggota suku, dan hubungan antara satu suku dengan suku lainnya. Berikut ini adalah beberapa kenyataan dasar masyarakat Arab sebelum datangnya Islam:

1) Tidak ada batas sejauh mana kaum bangsawan merasa bangga dengan garis keturunan dan kedudukan mereka dalam masyarakat

Bangsa Arab terobsesi dengan gagasan mempertahankan kemurnian darah mereka, sehingga mereka tidak mau menikah dengan ras lain. Islam mengakhiri hal itu, dengan memperjelas kepada bangsa Arab bahwa satu-satunya faktor yang membuat seseorang lebih unggul dari yang lain adalah ketakwaan dan amal saleh.

2) Bangsa Arab juga terobsesi dengan kefasihan berbicara dan kemurnian bahasa

Mungkin alasan mengapa puisi memiliki pengaruh kuat terhadap hati dan pikiran bangsa Arab adalah bahwa puisi merupakan cara paling indah untuk mengungkapkan dan merekam bagi generasi mendatang — jika sebuah puisi sangat bagus — fakta-fakta tentang garis keturunan mereka yang mulia. Maka tidak mengherankan jika masyarakat Arab menghasilkan begitu banyak penyair dan orator yang luar biasa. Satu bait puisi memiliki potensi untuk mengangkat atau menurunkan status sebuah kabilah. Jadi sebagaimana sebuah negara modern menjadi bersemangat ketika tim olahraga favoritnya tampil di level kelas dunia, sebuah kabilah Arab akan sangat bergembira jika salah satu anggotanya menjadi penyair yang terampil.

3) Perempuan diperlakukan seperti barang dagangan

Setidaknya di antara sebagian besar kabilah, perempuan berada pada kedudukan yang sama dengan barang dagangan, dalam artian yang sangat harfiah, karena mereka diwariskan sebagai harta warisan. Jika seorang laki-laki meninggal dunia, istrinya diwariskan kepada putra sulungnya — asalkan, tentu saja, ia bukan ibu kandungnya sendiri. Putra sulung itu kemudian berhak untuk menikahinya, atau bahkan menghalanginya

Politik, agama, ekonomi, dan kondisi umum bangsa Arab... 45
content_ar:
يتناول الجزء السابع الغزوات الأولى وسرايا الاستطلاع في بداية العهد المدني؛ ويُفصّل في أولى المواجهات بين المسلمين ومشركي مكة وكيف كانت ترسم معالم الاستراتيجية العسكرية النبوية.

ويدرس الصلابي هذه الغزوات من منظور استراتيجي يُبيّن كيف أن النبي صلى الله عليه وسلم كان يُحكم التخطيط العسكري ويُراعي حفظ الأنفس وتحقيق الأهداف الاستراتيجية في آن. ويُقرّر أن الإسلام لا يُقدّس الحرب لذاتها بل يعدّها وسيلةً لتحقيق السلام والعدل.
